summary: Graham argues there are two kinds of political moderates: intentional trimmers who aim for the midpoint, and accidental moderates who arrive there by thinking independently on each issue. Intentional moderates mirror ideologues by acquiring positions in bulk, while accidental moderates choose their own questions and answers and often face more criticism from both sides.
key_points: Intentional moderates cluster near the middle on every issue; accidental moderates have a wide spread of views that average out.
Both ideologues and intentional moderates adopt positions in bulk rather than independently.
Accidental moderates may not care about many issues that dominate partisan debates, making their politics hard to map.
Open accidental moderates endure attacks from both left and right and lack the support of an orthodox group.
Independent-mindedness about ideas is essential for strong intellectual work; if politics intersects your work, independence or mediocrity follows.
money_quotes: There are two distinct ways to be politically moderate: on purpose and by accident.
The defining quality of an ideologue, whether on the left or the right, is to acquire one's opinions in bulk.
It is not merely a manipulative rhetorical trick to say "if you're not with us, you're against us," but often simply false.
Openly being an accidental moderate requires the most courage of all, because you get attacked from both right and left.
But if the ideas you use in your work intersect with the politics of your time, you have two choices: be an accidental moderate, or be mediocre.
key_insight: Independent-minded thinkers become accidental moderates not by trimming but by reasoning issue by issue, and that independence is essential for serious work with ideas.
